Day 11: Lesson 11 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 11

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 9 • Week 3

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 11!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 9: LIFE ON THE ISLAND
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 2"
  • Practice grammar skills
  • Complete writing activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: LIFE ON THE ISLAND

Part 1 of 3

When Tom woke in the morning, he couldn't remember where he was. He sat up and looked around. A cool gray dawn filtered through the trees. The forest was perfectly still. Dew sparkled on every leaf and blade of grass. A thin ribbon of smoke rose straight up from the dying campfire.

Then the woods began to wake up. A bird called. Another answered. A woodpecker hammered somewhere in the distance. A little green worm crawled onto Tom's leg, and he sat stone-still, watching it, because if a measuring worm walks all the way across you, it means you're going to get new clothes.

Gradually the forest came alive—ants marching, a catbird singing, a blue jay flashing through the trees, a squirrel chattering at the boys from a branch. The sun broke through the canopy like golden spears.

Tom shook the other two pirates awake, and within minutes they were all stripped down and splashing in the shallow water of the sandbar, laughing and ducking each other. No one missed the little village sleeping in the distance across the wide water.

They came back to camp starving and got the fire roaring again. Huck found a cold spring nearby. Tom and Joe went to the riverbank with hooks and lines, and within minutes they had enough bass and catfish for a feast. They fried the fish with bacon and declared it the best breakfast in history.

After the meal, they explored the island. It was about three miles long and a quarter mile wide, separated from the far shore by a narrow channel. They swam every hour or so, and by afternoon they knew the island like the back of their hands.

But as the afternoon wore on and the excitement faded, a strange heaviness crept over them. They stopped talking. They stopped playing. They sat looking across the water at the distant town and thought about things they didn't want to think about.

Suddenly, a deep, dull boom echoed across the river.

Page 4

📚 Reading: LIFE ON THE ISLAND

Part 2 of 3

"What was that?" Joe whispered.

"I don't know," said Tom.

"It isn't thunder," said Huck.

They crept to the shore and peered through the bushes. The town's small ferryboat was in the river about a mile below the village. People crowded its deck. Then a great puff of white smoke burst from the boat's side, and after a pause, the same heavy boom rolled across the water.

"I know!" Tom cried. "Somebody's drowned! They shoot a cannon over the water—it's supposed to make the body come up."

"That's right," said Huck. "They did the same thing last summer when Bill Turner drowned."

"I wonder who it is," said Joe.

They watched the boat go back and forth. Then, slowly, it dawned on Tom.

Page 5

📚 Reading: LIFE ON THE ISLAND

Part 3 of 3

"Boys," he said, "I know who drowned. It's us!"

They felt like heroes. They were missed! They were mourned! People were crying over them! They were the talk of the whole town! It was glorious.

As night fell, the ferryboat gave up and went home. The pirates returned to camp, puffed up with pride. They caught more fish, cooked supper, and spent the evening imagining what the whole village must be saying about them.

But when the fire burned low and the darkness closed in, the pride faded. Tom and Joe couldn't stop thinking about certain people at home who were not having a good time right now. They grew quiet and miserable.

Joe timidly suggested that maybe they could go home—not right now, but maybe—

Tom shut him down instantly. "Go home? A pirate doesn't go home! We'd be laughed at!"

But later that night, after Joe and Huck had fallen asleep, Tom lay awake thinking. Carefully, quietly, he wrote something on two pieces of sycamore bark. He put one in his pocket and one in Joe's hat. Then he crept out of camp and disappeared into the night.

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. What do the boys hear booming across the river, and what does it mean?

2. How do the boys feel when they realize the town thinks they are dead?

3. At the end of the chapter, Tom sneaks away from camp and writes something on two pieces of sycamore bark. Why do you think the author doesn't tell us yet what Tom wrote? How does this create suspense?

Page 7

📝 Grammar Workshop

Verb Moods: Indicative, Imperative, Subjunctive

What is it?
Indicative states facts: 'Tom climbed the fence.' Imperative gives commands: 'Climb the fence!' Subjunctive expresses wishes or hypotheticals: 'If Tom were here, he would climb the fence.' Twain uses all three to create dialogue and narration.

Example:
"Indicative: 'Tom is clever.' / Imperative: 'Be clever!' / Subjunctive: 'If Tom were more careful, he wouldn't get caught.'"

✍️ Your Turn:

Write six sentences about today's chapter: two in indicative mood (statements), two in imperative mood (commands), and two in subjunctive mood (wishes or hypotheticals).

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 2

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Question 1: What is the main idea of this text?

0 / 30 words

Question 2: What is one interesting fact you learned?

0 / 30 words

Question 3: How does this information connect to Tom Sawyer's story?

0 / 30 words

Page 9

✏️ Writing Practice

Prompt:
Create a different ending for today's chapter. What else could have happened?

💡 Tips: Start with a topic sentence, include specific details, and end with a conclusion.

Day 12: Lesson 12 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 12

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 10 • Week 3

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 12!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 10: THEIR OWN FUNERAL
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 3"
  • Practice language skills
  • Complete journal activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: THEIR OWN FUNERAL

Part 1 of 3

Tom waded across the shallow sandbar, swam the deeper channel, and made his way through the dark woods to the riverbank opposite town. He slipped into the water again, swam to the ferryboat landing, and sneaked aboard a small rowboat tied to the stern. When the last ferry of the night crossed, Tom rode along underneath.

He crept through quiet streets to his aunt's house. A light was burning in the sitting room. He pressed himself against the door, lifted the latch, and eased the door open—just a crack.

Aunt Polly, Sid, Mary, and Joe Harper's mother sat together in the lamplight. They had been crying. They were talking about the boys.

"He wasn't a bad boy," Aunt Polly was saying, her voice breaking. "Only mischievous. Just high-spirited, that's all. He never meant any harm, and he was the kindest-hearted boy in the world." She started crying again.

"It was just the same with my Joe," Mrs. Harper sobbed. "Full of mischief, but as sweet and generous as could be. And to think I whipped him for drinking that cream, when I was the one who threw it out! I'll never see him again."

"The Lord giveth and the Lord taketh away," Aunt Polly said. "But it's so hard. Only last Saturday, Tom filled the cat full of that terrible medicine, and I smacked him—and now I wish I could do it all over. I'd hug him instead."

Tom was sniffling under the bed by now, feeling so sorry for himself—and for his aunt—that he could barely stand it. He learned that the funeral would be preached on Sunday morning, since no bodies had been found. The whole town assumed the boys had drowned while rafting across the river.

After Mrs. Harper left, and Aunt Polly finally went to bed, Tom crept out. He stood over her sleeping form for a long moment. He took the piece of sycamore bark from his pocket and started to place it beside her candle—then stopped. A better idea had struck him. A magnificent idea.

Page 4

📚 Reading: THEIR OWN FUNERAL

Part 2 of 3

He put the bark back in his pocket, bent down and kissed his aunt's forehead, and slipped out into the night.

He made it back to Jackson's Island by dawn. He found his friends still sleeping and collapsed beside them. When they woke, he told them everything—well, almost everything. He told them the town thought they were dead. He told them about the funeral. And then he told them his idea.

Their eyes went wide.

"Tom Sawyer," said Joe, "that is the greatest idea I have ever heard."

Sunday morning came. The church bell tolled slowly—not its usual bright ring, but a single, mournful note. The villagers gathered in silence, dressed in black. The little church had never been so full.

Aunt Polly entered, followed by Sid and Mary. Mrs. Harper came next with her family. They were led to the front pew while the whole congregation stood in respect.

The minister spoke about the lost boys—their good hearts, their sweet natures, their playful spirits. He told stories about them that made people laugh through their tears. By the time he was done, every person in the church was weeping openly. Even the minister himself was crying.

The church door opened, just a little. Every head turned.

Page 5

📚 Reading: THEIR OWN FUNERAL

Part 3 of 3

Three boys came marching up the aisle: Tom in the lead, Joe next, and Huck bringing up the rear in his usual collection of rags, looking like he'd rather be anywhere else on earth.

They had been hiding in the church gallery the whole time, listening to their own funeral.

Aunt Polly, Mary, and Mrs. Harper threw themselves on the boys, smothering them with hugs and kisses and tears. Huck stood off to the side, uncomfortable and embarrassed, until Tom said: "Aunt Polly, somebody ought to be glad to see Huck, too."

"And so I am!" she cried, and wrapped him up in a hug that made him squirm.

The minister shouted: "Praise God! Now—sing! And put your hearts in it!"

And they did. The old hymn rang out so strong it shook the rafters, and Tom Sawyer the Pirate looked around at the faces of every kid in town—all of them green with envy—and decided that this was the proudest moment of his entire life.

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. What does Tom learn while hiding under Aunt Polly's bed?

2. What does Tom do just before leaving his aunt's house?

3. The boys walk into their own funeral and shock the entire congregation. Why is this moment the "proudest moment" of Tom's life? What does this tell us about what Tom values most?

Page 7

🗣️ Language Skills

Multiple Meaning Words (Homonyms)

Learn:
Many words have multiple meanings depending on context. In Tom Sawyer, words like 'play,' 'work,' 'run,' 'fence,' and 'beat' can mean different things. Context determines which meaning fits.

Example:
"'Play' can mean: (1) have fun, (2) perform in a show, (3) compete in a game. Context tells you which meaning applies."

✍️ Your Turn:

Find three words in today's chapter that have multiple meanings. For each word: write the sentence from the text, identify which meaning is used, and write a new sentence using a different meaning of the same word.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 3

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Question 1: What is the main idea of this text?

0 / 30 words

Question 2: What is one interesting fact you learned?

0 / 30 words

Question 3: How does this information connect to Tom Sawyer's story?

0 / 30 words

Page 9

📔 Journal Response

Reflect on today's reading:
What emotion did you feel most while reading today's chapter? Describe why the story made you feel that way.

💭 This is your space to explore your ideas and reactions to the story.

Day 13: Lesson 13 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 13

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 11 • Week 3

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 13!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 11: TOM'S DREAM TRICK
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 3"
  • Practice grammar skills
  • Complete writing activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: TOM'S DREAM TRICK

Part 1 of 3

At breakfast on Monday, Aunt Polly was showering Tom with love, but underneath it, something was bothering her.

"Well, Tom," she finally said, "I don't say it wasn't a fine joke to keep everybody worrying for a whole week while you boys had your fun. But it is a shame you could be so hard-hearted. If you could float across on a log to attend your own funeral, you could have floated across to let me know you weren't dead."

"You could have done that, Tom," said Mary. "I believe you would have, if you'd thought of it."

"Would you, Tom?" Aunt Polly's eyes were hopeful. "Would you have, if you'd thought of it?"

"I—well, I don't know. It would have spoiled everything."

Aunt Polly's face fell. "Tom, I hoped you loved me enough to think of it. It would have been something if you'd even cared enough to consider it."

"Now, Auntie, it's not that bad," Mary said. "That's just Tom's way—he's always in such a rush he never thinks ahead."

"I wish I had thought of it," Tom said, and he meant it. "But I dreamed about you. That's something."

"A cat does that much. But it's better than nothing. What did you dream?"

Tom had to think fast. "Well—Wednesday night I dreamed you were sitting right over there by the bed. And Sid was by the woodbox, and Mary next to him."

"Well, so we were! That's where we always sit. Go on."

"And I dreamed that Joe Harper's mother was here."

Page 4

📚 Reading: TOM'S DREAM TRICK

Part 2 of 3

"She was! Did you dream anything else?"

"Oh, lots. But it's kind of fuzzy—"

"It seems like—the wind blew the—the—"

"What? What did the wind blow?"

"It blew the candle!"

"And it seems like you said the door was open—"

"As sure as I'm sitting here, I did! Mary, didn't I?"

"And then you made Sid go shut it."

"Well, I never! Go on, Tom!"

"Then you said I wasn't bad, just mischievous, and not any more responsible than—a colt, or something."

"That's exactly what I said! Goodness gracious, go on!"

"And then you started crying."

Page 5

📚 Reading: TOM'S DREAM TRICK

Part 3 of 3

"And then Mrs. Harper cried too, and said she wished she hadn't whipped Joe for the cream—"

"Tom! It's like you were right there in the room! What else?"

Tom rolled out every detail he could remember from hiding under the bed. Aunt Polly's eyes grew wider with every word. By the end she was fully convinced it had been a genuine prophetic dream—maybe even sent by angels.

"And then I thought you prayed for me," Tom said quietly. "And I could hear every word. And you went to bed, and I was so sorry—I took a piece of sycamore bark and wrote on it, 'We aren't dead—we're only off being pirates.' And I put it on the table by the candle. And then you looked so peaceful, sleeping there, that I bent down and kissed you."

"Did you, Tom? Did you really?"

"I forgive you everything for that!" She crushed him in a hug. "Even though it was only a dream."

"Shut up, Sid," Tom muttered, catching his brother's skeptical look.

After Tom left for school, Aunt Polly's heart struggled between joy and suspicion. She went to the closet and got out the ragged jacket Tom had worn on his pirate adventure. She held it, arguing with herself: "I won't look. It's a good lie—a kind lie. I won't let it grieve me."

But she looked. She reached into the pocket—and pulled out a piece of sycamore bark.

She read it through tears that wouldn't stop falling. It said: WE AIN'T DEAD—WE ARE ONLY OFF BEING PIRATES.

"I could forgive that boy," she whispered, "if he'd committed a million sins."

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. How does Tom explain knowing all the details of the conversation Aunt Polly had with Mrs. Harper?

2. What does Aunt Polly find in the pocket of Tom's old jacket?

3. Aunt Polly calls the sycamore bark message proof that Tom loved her. Tom originally planned to leave it but changed his mind. Does Tom deserve the credit Aunt Polly gives him? Why or why not?

Page 7

📝 Grammar Workshop

Adjective and Adverb Clauses

What is it?
An adjective clause modifies a noun: 'Tom, who was very clever, tricked his friends.' An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb: 'Tom worked quickly because he wanted to finish.' Both add detail to sentences.

Example:
"Adjective: 'The fence that Tom painted stood in the yard.' / Adverb: 'Tom ran when he saw Aunt Polly.'"

✍️ Your Turn:

Write four sentences about today's reading: two with adjective clauses (use who, which, that) and two with adverb clauses (use when, because, if, although).

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 3

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Question 1: What is the main idea of this text?

0 / 30 words

Question 2: What is one interesting fact you learned?

0 / 30 words

Question 3: How does this information connect to Tom Sawyer's story?

0 / 30 words

Page 9

✏️ Writing Practice

Prompt:
Write a paragraph describing a time when you had to do a chore you didn't want to do. How did you handle it? What did you learn?

💡 Tips: Start with a topic sentence, include specific details, and end with a conclusion.

Day 14: Lesson 14 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 14

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 12 • Week 3

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 14!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 12: MAKING UP WITH BECKY
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 3"
  • Practice language skills
  • Complete journal activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: MAKING UP WITH BECKY

Part 1 of 3

Something about Aunt Polly's kiss that morning lifted all the weight off Tom's shoulders. He felt light and happy for the first time in weeks. On his way to school, he ran into Becky Thatcher.

"Becky, I acted awful mean the other day, and I'm so sorry. I won't ever do it again. Please—can we make up?"

She stopped and gave him a cold stare. "I'll thank you to keep away from me, Tom Sawyer. I'm never speaking to you again."

She tossed her braids and walked on.

Tom was so stunned he couldn't even think of a comeback. His good mood evaporated. He stomped into the schoolyard, furious.

For the rest of the morning, Tom noticed Becky laughing and running around the yard, always managing to end up close to him, clearly trying to get his attention. He pretended not to notice. When he saw her looking his way, he turned and talked loudly to Amy Lawrence instead.

Becky's smile faded. She tried to look cheerful, but the light went out of her face. She drifted away.

Meanwhile, something dangerous was happening. The teacher, Mr. Dobbins, kept a mysterious book in his desk—locked up, always hidden. Every kid in school was dying to know what it was. On this particular day, as Becky passed the teacher's desk, she noticed the key was in the lock.

She glanced around. The room was empty. She turned the key and opened the book. The title page said Professor Somebody's Anatomy. She didn't know what that meant, so she started flipping through it—and came upon a picture of a human body, completely naked.

At that exact moment, Tom walked through the door. Becky slammed the book shut in a panic—and tore the picture right down the middle. She shoved the book back in the desk, turned the key, and burst into tears.

"Tom Sawyer! You are the meanest person alive, sneaking up on people!"

"How was I supposed to know you were looking at anything?"

Page 4

📚 Reading: MAKING UP WITH BECKY

Part 2 of 3

"You're going to tell on me, and I'll get whipped, and I've never been whipped in school!"

She stormed out, crying.

Tom stood there, bewildered. "What a fuss over nothing," he thought. "Girls are so thin-skinned. I'm not going to tell old Dobbins. But he'll figure it out on his own. He'll ask every kid in the room, and when he gets to Becky, she won't be able to hide it—girls can never keep a straight face. She's going to get it. Too bad for her."

He paused. "But she would have let ME get in trouble without thinking twice. All right, let her sweat."

But he didn't feel right about it.

That afternoon, Mr. Dobbins took the book from his desk. He settled into his chair to read it—and found the torn page.

Every student sat up straight. Tom glanced at Becky. She looked like a trapped rabbit.

Mr. Dobbins stood and faced the room. "Who tore this book?"

Dead silence. You could hear a pin drop.

"Benjamin Rogers, did you tear this book?"

"Joseph Harper, did you?"

One by one, the teacher worked his way through the class. Tom's heart pounded harder with each name. The teacher turned to the girls.

Page 5

📚 Reading: MAKING UP WITH BECKY

Part 3 of 3

Becky's face went white. Tom looked at her. Her eyes were wide with terror, her hands pressed together.

"Did you tear this book?"

Her lips moved, but no sound came out.

Tom leaped to his feet. "I did it!"

The whole school stared in shock.

Mr. Dobbins said nothing for a long, terrible moment. Then: "Come here."

Tom walked forward. He took the worst whipping Mr. Dobbins had ever given, plus two hours of detention after school. He didn't make a sound.

And the whole time, he could feel Becky Thatcher's eyes on him—shining with gratitude and something that looked an awful lot like love.

Tom went to bed that night still stinging from the whipping, but Becky's last words kept playing in his head like music:

"Tom, how could you be so noble!"

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. Why does Mr. Dobbins become angry with the class?

2. What does Tom do when Mr. Dobbins asks Becky if she tore the book?

3. Tom takes a whipping he doesn't deserve to protect Becky. How is this moment different from his earlier "showing off" to impress her? What has changed about Tom's feelings or motivations?

Page 7

🗣️ Language Skills

Figurative Language: Similes and Metaphors

Learn:
A simile compares using 'like' or 'as': 'Tom ran like the wind.' A metaphor says something IS something else: 'Tom was a lightning bolt.' Twain uses both to create vivid imagery. Similes are easier to spot; metaphors are more subtle.

Example:
"Simile: 'The fence stretched like an endless desert.' / Metaphor: 'Tom's heart was a stone when he saw the fence.'"

✍️ Your Turn:

Find two examples of figurative language (similes or metaphors) in today's reading. Explain what is being compared and what image the comparison creates. Then write your own simile and metaphor about something from the chapter.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 3

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Which character do you relate to most in this chapter? Explain why.

Day 16: Lesson 16 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 16

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 13 • Week 4

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 16!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 13: EXAMINATION DAY
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 4"
  • Practice grammar skills
  • Complete writing activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: EXAMINATION DAY

Part 1 of 3

Vacation was coming, and Mr. Dobbins was getting meaner by the day. He seemed to take a vicious pleasure in whipping students for the smallest mistakes. The younger kids spent their days in terror and their nights plotting revenge.

They finally came up with a plan—and it was a beauty.

The sign-painter's boy was in on it. His father rented a room to Mr. Dobbins, so the boy had his own reasons for wanting revenge. The plan required the teacher's wife to be out of town, and it required Mr. Dobbins to be drunk—neither of which was unusual.

On Examination Evening, the schoolhouse was packed. Parents, town leaders, and visitors filled every seat. The teacher sat on a raised platform with a blackboard behind him, looking only slightly unsteady. Rows of students sat on a temporary stage—boys scrubbed raw and miserable in stiff clothes, girls in white dresses with ribbons in their hair.

The performances began. A tiny boy recited a poem with robotic gestures and nearly fainted from stage fright. A girl lisped her way through "Mary Had a Little Lamb." Tom Sawyer stepped forward with grand confidence and launched into a dramatic speech—"Give me liberty or give me death!"—but froze halfway through. Stage fright hit him like a wall. His legs shook, his throat closed. The audience stared in painful silence. He gave up and slunk off the stage.

Page 4

📚 Reading: EXAMINATION DAY

Part 2 of 3

More recitations followed. Then came the spelling bee, the Latin class, and finally the main event—original essays by the older girls. Every single essay, no matter the topic, ended with the same kind of preachy moral lesson. The audience clapped politely and pretended to be moved.

Then Mr. Dobbins stood to lead the geography class. He turned to the blackboard and began drawing a map of America. But his hand was unsteady from drink, and the map came out crooked and wrong. A ripple of laughter ran through the crowd. He erased and tried again—worse. The laughter grew louder.

He concentrated harder, determined to get it right. He didn't notice what was happening above him.

In the ceiling, there was a small opening—a trapdoor to the attic. Through it, slowly, came a cat. She was tied to a rope and lowered inch by inch, a rag around her head to keep her quiet. She dangled and twisted, clawing at the air, getting closer and closer to the teacher's head.

The audience was shaking with laughter now. The cat was six inches from Mr. Dobbins—four inches—two—

Page 5

📚 Reading: EXAMINATION DAY

Part 3 of 3

She grabbed his wig with both paws and was yanked back up into the attic, taking the wig with her.

The teacher's bald head gleamed in the lamplight. The sign-painter's boy had gilded it while the teacher napped. It shone like a mirror.

The room exploded. The meeting was over. The boys were avenged. And vacation had begun.

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. What is the boys' plan for getting revenge on Mr. Dobbins?

2. Why does the audience laugh even harder when the cat removes Mr. Dobbins's wig?

3. The chapter describes students performing recitations, essays, and demonstrations on Examination Day. How is Examination Day different from how we test students today? Which approach do you think is better, and why?

Page 7

📝 Grammar Workshop

Parallel Structure

What is it?
Parallel structure means using the same grammatical form for items in a list or series. 'Tom liked swimming, fishing, and exploring' (all gerunds). Twain uses parallel structure to create rhythm: 'Tom was clever, brave, and mischievous.'

Example:
"Parallel: 'Tom ran, jumped, and shouted.' / Not parallel: 'Tom ran, jumped, and was shouting.'"

✍️ Your Turn:

Find an example of parallel structure in today's chapter (or create one if you can't find one). Then write three sentences using parallel structure to describe characters, events, or settings from the story.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 4

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

0 / 30 words

0 / 30 words

Page 9

Write a persuasive paragraph explaining whether you think Tom's behavior in this chapter was clever or wrong.

💡 Tips: Start with a topic sentence, include specific details, and end with a conclusion.

Day 17: Lesson 17 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 17

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 14 • Week 4

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 17!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 14: A MISERABLE SUMMER
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 4"
  • Practice language skills
  • Complete journal activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: A MISERABLE SUMMER

Part 1 of 3

Tom joined the Cadets of Temperance, attracted by their fancy uniforms. He promised to stop swearing and smoking. Naturally, the moment he promised not to do these things, they were all he could think about. He quit after two days.

The Fourth of July was a disappointment—it rained, and a visiting senator turned out to be a normal-sized man, which was deeply unsatisfying.

A circus came to town. The boys played circus in the backyard for three days and then got bored.

Becky Thatcher left for the summer to visit relatives in another town. Without her, nothing had any flavor.

Page 4

📚 Reading: A MISERABLE SUMMER

Part 2 of 3

And underneath it all, like a toothache that never stopped, was the murder. The secret pressed on Tom's conscience every single day.

Then came the measles. Tom was bedridden for two weeks, completely cut off from the world. When he finally dragged himself outside, everything had changed. A religious revival had swept through St. Petersburg. Everyone had "gotten religion"—even the boys. Tom went looking for a friendly face and found Joe Harper reading the Bible. Ben Rogers was delivering charity baskets. Jim Hollis told Tom his measles were God's warning to shape up. Even Huckleberry Finn greeted him with a Bible verse.

Tom's heart sank. He went home and crawled into bed, convinced he was the only lost soul left in town.

That night, a terrible thunderstorm struck. Lightning split the sky, thunder shook the house, and rain lashed the windows. Tom pulled the covers over his head, certain that all of this was aimed directly at him—that God had finally had enough of Tom Sawyer.

Page 5

📚 Reading: A MISERABLE SUMMER

Part 3 of 3

But the storm passed, and he was still alive.

Then the measles came back. He spent three more weeks in bed. When he finally recovered, the revival was over. Joe Harper was back to mischief. Ben Rogers had gone back to normal. Huck was eating a stolen melon behind a fence.

Everything was just as it had been—except that the murder trial was about to begin.

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. Why does Tom join the Cadets of Temperance?

2. What happens to the town's religious revival by the time Tom recovers from his second bout of measles?

3. Tom discovers that "to promise not to do a thing is the surest way in the world to make a body want to go and do that very thing." How does this connect to the "great law of human nature" Tom discovered in Chapter 2 (the whitewashing)? Are these the same idea or different?

Page 7

🗣️ Language Skills

Idioms and Expressions

Learn:
Idioms are expressions that mean something different from their literal words. Tom Sawyer is full of 1800s American idioms: 'play hookey' (skip school), 'lay for' (wait to ambush), 'take a dare' (accept a challenge). Understanding idioms helps you understand the culture and time period.

Example:
"'Get hold of' doesn't mean literally grabbing—it means 'catch' or 'find.' 'Full of the Old Scratch' means mischievous (Old Scratch = the devil)."

✍️ Your Turn:

Find three idioms or unusual expressions in today's chapter. For each: write the expression, explain what it literally says, explain what it actually means, and write a modern equivalent expression.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 4

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

0 / 30 words

0 / 30 words

Page 9

📔 Journal Response

Reflect on today's reading:
How would today's chapter be different if it took place in modern times?

💭 This is your space to explore your ideas and reactions to the story.

Day 18: Lesson 18 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 18

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 15 • Week 4

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 18!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 15: THE TRIAL
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 4"
  • Practice grammar skills
  • Complete writing activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: THE TRIAL

Part 1 of 3

The trial of Muff Potter consumed the entire village. It was all anyone talked about. Tom couldn't escape it—every conversation, every rumor, every whispered opinion was like a needle in his conscience. He pulled Huck to a private spot to make sure they were still in agreement.

"Huck, have you told anybody?"

"Told anybody about what?"

"Of course I haven't. Not one word. Why?"

"I was just making sure. I'm scared, Huck."

"Me too. If Injun Joe ever found out—we wouldn't last two days."

They swore their oath again, just to be safe.

The trial dragged on for days. Witness after witness testified against Muff Potter. They described finding the knife, seeing Potter wash blood off himself in the middle of the night, his suspicious behavior. Potter's own lawyer barely asked a single question. The courtroom grew restless—did the defense attorney plan to just give up?

Tom and Huck haunted the edges of the courthouse, drawn by the same horrible fascination. Every day, they heard worse news. The evidence was piling up. The verdict seemed certain.

Page 4

📚 Reading: THE TRIAL

Part 2 of 3

On the last day of the trial, the courtroom was packed. Potter sat in chains, pale and broken, his eyes empty. Injun Joe sat nearby, calm and expressionless as stone.

The prosecution rested its case. Potter's lawyer stood and said something that shocked the room: "Call Thomas Sawyer to the stand."

Every eye turned to Tom as he walked forward. He looked terrified. He was terrified. He took the oath and sat down.

"Thomas Sawyer, where were you on the night of June seventeenth, around midnight?"

Tom looked at Injun Joe's face—hard, dark, and still as iron. His tongue wouldn't work. The audience leaned forward. Silence stretched like a wire about to snap.

Finally, Tom managed to push words out, barely above a whisper: "In the graveyard."

"A little louder, please. Where were you?"

Injun Joe's eyes flickered—just slightly.

"Were you near the grave of Hoss Williams?"

Page 5

📚 Reading: THE TRIAL

Part 3 of 3

"As near as I am to you right now."

"Yes, sir. Behind the elm trees."

"Was anyone with you?"

"Tell us everything that happened. Tell it in your own way. Don't skip anything."

Tom began slowly, haltingly. But as the story poured out of him, his voice grew stronger. Every person in that courtroom stopped breathing. They sat frozen, mouths open, eyes fixed on this boy who was describing, in vivid and terrible detail, everything that had happened in the graveyard that night.

"—and as the doctor swung the headboard and hit Muff Potter, Injun Joe jumped up with the knife and—"

Injun Joe hurled himself at the nearest window. Glass exploded outward. Before anyone could move, he was through it and gone—running, leaping over a fence, disappearing into the night.

The courtroom erupted into chaos. But Muff Potter was free.

And Tom Sawyer was a hero—again.

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. What is the most surprising moment at Muff Potter's trial?

2. What happens immediately after Tom describes seeing Injun Joe stab the doctor?

3. Tom must have known that testifying would put him in danger from Injun Joe. Why does he do it anyway? What has finally become stronger than his fear?

Page 7

📝 Grammar Workshop

Restrictive vs. Non-Restrictive Clauses

What is it?
A restrictive clause is essential to the meaning and needs no commas: 'The boy who painted the fence is Tom.' A non-restrictive clause adds extra information and uses commas: 'Tom, who is Aunt Polly's nephew, painted the fence.'

Example:
"Restrictive: 'The chapter that we read today was exciting.' / Non-restrictive: 'Chapter 10, which we read today, was exciting.'"

✍️ Your Turn:

Write four sentences about today's chapter: two with restrictive clauses (no commas) and two with non-restrictive clauses (with commas). Label each type.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 4

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Question 1: What is the main idea of this text?

0 / 30 words

Question 2: What is one interesting fact you learned?

0 / 30 words

Question 3: How does this information connect to Tom Sawyer's story?

0 / 30 words

Page 9

✏️ Writing Practice

Prompt:
Write a character analysis of one person from today's chapter. What are their personality traits? What motivates them?

💡 Tips: Start with a topic sentence, include specific details, and end with a conclusion.

Day 19: Lesson 19 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 19

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 16 • Week 4

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 19!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 16: TREASURE HUNTING
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 4"
  • Practice language skills
  • Complete journal activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: TREASURE HUNTING

Part 1 of 3

Tom was the golden boy of St. Petersburg once more. His name appeared in the local paper. People said he might be president someday.

But at night, the dreams came. Injun Joe was in every one of them—always lurking, always watching, always with murder in his eyes. Huck was just as frightened. Tom had told the lawyer everything, which meant Huck's share in the secret might eventually come out.

A detective came from St. Louis, poked around town, shook his head wisely, and went home without finding anything useful. Injun Joe had vanished.

Slowly, day by day, Tom's fear faded. He would never feel completely safe until Injun Joe was caught or confirmed dead. But boys' minds don't stay in one place for long, and before he knew it, Tom was consumed by a new obsession: buried treasure.

He found Huck and presented the idea.

"Where do we dig?" Huck asked.

"That's the thing—treasure is always buried in specific places. Under dead trees, in haunted houses, on islands."

Page 4

📚 Reading: TREASURE HUNTING

Part 2 of 3

"Robbers, obviously."

"If I had treasure, I'd spend it."

"So would I. But robbers always hide it and then forget where they put it. Or they die. Then somebody finds an old yellow map with mysterious markings, and after about a week of working out the clues, they find the treasure."

Huck was sold. They grabbed a pick and a shovel and hiked three miles to a dead tree on a hillside. They dug for hours. Nothing. They tried a different spot. Nothing. They tried yet another.

"Do they always bury it this deep?" Huck asked, wiping sweat from his face.

"Not always. I think we've got the wrong place."

They tried two more spots before Tom slapped his forehead. "Wait—you have to find where the shadow of the dead branch falls at midnight, and that's where you dig!"

Page 5

📚 Reading: TREASURE HUNTING

Part 3 of 3

"Then we've wasted the whole day! We have to come back at night."

They hid their tools and came back that night by moonlight. It was eerie work—the wind moaning through the trees, the darkness pressing in. They dug where the shadow fell. Still nothing.

"I know what we should do," Tom said at last. "We should try the haunted house."

Huck's face went pale. "Tom, I don't like haunted houses. They're worse than graveyards. Dead people might talk to you, but ghosts sneak up behind you and breathe on your neck."

"Ghosts only come out at night. We'd go in the daytime."

Huck thought it over. "All right. But I don't like it."

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. According to Tom, where is treasure usually buried?

2. Why do the boys decide they need to come back to the dead tree at midnight?

3. Tom and Huck are full of superstitions about treasure hunting — incantations, witches, midnight shadows. How do these superstitions affect the way they approach the treasure hunt? Do the superstitions help them or hold them back?

Page 7

🗣️ Language Skills

Greek and Latin Roots: port, ject, dict, scrib/script

Learn:
Many English words come from Greek and Latin roots. port (carry), ject (throw), dict (speak), scrib/script (write). Understanding roots helps you decode unfamiliar words. Examples: transport, project, predict, describe, scripture.

Example:
"'Transport' = trans (across) + port (carry) = carry across. 'Project' = pro (forward) + ject (throw) = throw forward."

✍️ Your Turn:

Learn these four roots and their meanings. Find or create five words using these roots (use a dictionary if needed). For each word: identify the root, write the definition, and use it in a sentence about Tom Sawyer.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 4

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Question 1: What is the main idea of this text?

0 / 30 words

Question 2: What is one interesting fact you learned?

0 / 30 words

Question 3: How does this information connect to Tom Sawyer's story?

0 / 30 words

Page 9

📔 Journal Response

Reflect on today's reading:
What was the most interesting or surprising thing that happened in today's chapter? Why?

💭 This is your space to explore your ideas and reactions to the story.

Day 21: Lesson 21 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 21

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 17 • Week 5

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 21!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 17: THE HAUNTED HOUSE
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 5"
  • Practice grammar skills
  • Complete writing activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: THE HAUNTED HOUSE

Part 1 of 3

On Saturday, the boys hiked to the old abandoned house on Still-House Branch. The place was a wreck—fences gone, weeds everywhere, chimney crumbling, windows empty and dark. They crept inside, hearts hammering, speaking in whispers.

The ground floor was a ruin: floorboards rotting, fireplace cold, cobwebs hanging in thick curtains from every beam. They explored carefully, jumping at every creak. After a while, their courage grew. They even climbed the rickety stairs to the second floor, where they found nothing but an empty closet and more decay.

They were about to head back down to start digging when Tom grabbed Huck's arm.

"Stay still—they're coming through the door—"

The boys dropped flat on their bellies and pressed their eyes to cracks in the floorboards. Two men walked in below them. One was a ragged, mean-looking stranger. The other appeared to be an old, deaf Spaniard who'd been spotted around town a few times—dark skin, white whiskers, green glasses, a wide-brimmed hat.

But when the "Spaniard" spoke, the boys nearly choked with terror. The voice belonged to Injun Joe.

"No," said the ragged man, "I've thought it over, and I don't like it. It's too dangerous."

"Dangerous?" Joe growled. "Don't be a coward."

They talked about some kind of "job" they had planned—something risky that involved another town upriver. Then Joe said: "We'll do that job after I've had one more look around this town. Then we head for Texas."

The men ate lunch. Then, to the boys' combined horror and fascination, Joe said: "We might as well bury the money while we're here—bury it deep."

His partner went to the far wall, knelt down, lifted a hearthstone, and pulled out a bag that clinked heavily. He counted out some coins for each of them and handed the rest to Joe, who was already on his knees in the corner, digging with his bowie knife.

Page 4

📚 Reading: THE HAUNTED HOUSE

Part 2 of 3

The boys above could barely contain themselves. Six hundred and fifty dollars in silver! Right there!

Joe's knife struck something solid.

"What's that?" said the stranger.

"Half-rotten plank—no, it's a box." Joe broke through the wood and reached inside. "It's money!"

He pulled out a handful of gold coins. The men stared at them, then at each other.

"There's thousands here," Joe whispered. "This must be from Murrel's gang—they used to operate around here."

"Now we don't need to do that other job."

Joe's face darkened. "You don't know me. That job isn't about money. It's about revenge. I'll need your help. After it's done—then Texas."

"All right. What do we do with all this gold? Bury it again?"

"Yes." The boys above nearly burst with joy. "No—wait." Their hearts dropped. "That pick and shovel over there—they've got fresh dirt on them. Who brought them here? Who's been digging?"

Ice ran through Tom's veins. Joe was staring at the boys' tools, which they'd left in the corner.

Page 5

📚 Reading: THE HAUNTED HOUSE

Part 3 of 3

"We'll take the money to my other hideout," Joe said. "Number Two—under the cross."

"Not Number One—too risky. Number Two."

Joe stood and moved from window to window, peering out. "Whoever brought those tools could be upstairs right now."

The boys stopped breathing. Joe put his hand on his knife and started for the staircase. The boys were about to dive for the closet when—CRACK—the rotten stairs collapsed under Joe's weight. He crashed to the ground floor in a shower of splintered wood.

His partner said: "Forget it. If anyone's up there, let them stay. It'll be dark soon—let them follow us if they dare."

Joe cursed and grumbled, but agreed. The two men gathered the treasure box and slipped out into the fading light.

Tom and Huck lay on the floor for a long, long time before they dared to move. When they finally crept down and out of the house, they were shaking.

"Revenge," Tom whispered. "Who do you think he means?"

"I don't know, but—Tom, what if he means us?"

They walked home in grim silence, sick with the knowledge that Injun Joe was somewhere close, with a knife and a grudge and a box full of gold.

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. What terrifying discovery do Tom and Huck make while hiding upstairs in the haunted house?

2. Why does Injun Joe become suspicious that someone else has been in the haunted house?

3. Injun Joe says the treasure needs to go to "Number Two — under the cross" and mentions that his planned "job" is about revenge, not money. What do these details tell us about what might happen next in the story? Make a prediction and explain your reasoning.

Page 7

📝 Grammar Workshop

Colons and Dashes for Emphasis

What is it?
A colon introduces a list, explanation, or quotation: 'Tom had one goal: freedom.' A dash creates emphasis or interruption: 'Tom—always clever—found a solution.' Both add style to writing.

Example:
"Colon: 'Tom needed three things: courage, wit, and luck.' / Dash: 'The fence—all thirty yards of it—needed whitewashing.'"

✍️ Your Turn:

Write four sentences about today's reading: two using colons (for lists or explanations) and two using dashes (for emphasis or interruptions).

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 5

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Question 1: What is the main idea of this text?

0 / 30 words

Question 2: What is one interesting fact you learned?

0 / 30 words

0 / 30 words

Page 9

💡 Tips: Start with a topic sentence, include specific details, and end with a conclusion.

Day 22: Lesson 22 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 22

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 18 • Week 5

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 22!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 18: TRACKING NUMBER TWO
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 5"
  • Practice language skills
  • Complete journal activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: TRACKING NUMBER TWO

Part 1 of 3

The treasure haunted Tom's dreams—but so did Injun Joe. The gold was out there somewhere, hidden in a place called "Number Two, under the cross." Tom had to find it. But he also had to avoid getting killed.

He and Huck talked it through. "Number Two" had to be a room number—probably in one of the town's two taverns. Tom went to investigate. At the better tavern, Room Number Two was occupied by a young lawyer. At the cheaper tavern, the owner's son said Room Number Two was always locked. Nobody went in or out except late at night. He didn't know why. Most people figured it was haunted.

"That's our place," Tom told Huck. "The back door of that room opens into the alley. You get every old key you can find, and I'll get Aunt Polly's keys. The first dark night, we'll try them all."

"What if we run into Injun Joe?"

"It'll be dark—he probably won't see us. And if he does—well, we run."

"I don't like this one bit, Tom."

"I don't love it either. But think about that gold."

Page 4

📚 Reading: TRACKING NUMBER TWO

Part 2 of 3

They tried on Thursday night. The alley behind the tavern was pitch dark—perfect. Tom crept up to the door, heart slamming against his ribs. He tried one key. It didn't turn. He tried another—a third—

Then his hand closed on the doorknob, and the door swung open. It wasn't even locked.

He eased inside, peeled back the towel he'd wrapped around his lantern, and—

Something moved on the floor. A body. An arm. Tom's light fell on a face—Injun Joe, passed out drunk, sprawled on the floor in a puddle of whiskey.

Tom didn't breathe. He didn't think. He ran.

He burst out of the alley like a bullet and didn't stop until he found Huck three blocks away.

"Run!" he gasped. "Run!"

Page 5

📚 Reading: TRACKING NUMBER TWO

Part 3 of 3

They didn't stop until they reached an abandoned shed on the edge of town. Rain began hammering the roof as they crouched inside, panting.

"Tom, what happened?"

"He's in there. Injun Joe. Asleep on the floor—drunk."

"Did you see the box?"

"I didn't wait to look around! I saw him and I ran."

They agreed to keep watch on the tavern. Every night, Huck would patrol the area. If he ever saw Injun Joe leave, he would come straight to Tom's window and signal. Then they'd make their move.

Huck took the first watch that night. And the next night. And the next. But Injun Joe never appeared.

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. How does Tom figure out that "Number Two" is a room in a tavern?

2. What does Tom find when he sneaks into Room Number Two?

3. Tom and Huck take enormous risks to track down the treasure — sneaking into a room where a murderer is sleeping. Is the treasure worth the danger? What would you do in their situation?

Page 7

🗣️ Language Skills

Connotation vs. Denotation

Learn:
Denotation is a word's literal dictionary meaning. Connotation is the feeling or association the word creates. 'Cheap' and 'inexpensive' both mean low-cost (denotation), but 'cheap' suggests poor quality (negative connotation) while 'inexpensive' is neutral. Twain chooses words carefully for their connotations.

Example:
"'Childish' (negative) vs. 'youthful' (positive) both relate to being young, but have different connotations."

✍️ Your Turn:

Find five descriptive words in today's reading (adjectives or adverbs). For each: write the denotation (literal meaning) and the connotation (feeling it creates—positive, negative, or neutral). Explain how the connotation affects your understanding of the character or scene.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 5

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Day 23: Lesson 23 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 23

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 19 • Week 5

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 23!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 19: THE PICNIC
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 5"
  • Practice grammar skills
  • Complete writing activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: THE PICNIC

Part 1 of 3

On Friday morning, Tom got the best possible news: Becky Thatcher's family had returned to town. Even better, Becky's mother had agreed to let her have the long-promised picnic the very next day.

Tom was ecstatic. By Saturday morning, a rowdy, laughing crowd of children gathered at Judge Thatcher's house and paraded through town to the ferry landing. The old steamboat carried them three miles downriver to a wooded hollow at the base of a steep, rocky hill.

The kids scattered into the woods—chasing each other, eating from picnic baskets, playing in the creek. By late afternoon, someone shouted: "Who's ready for the cave?"

Everyone was. They grabbed candles and scrambled up the hillside to McDougal's Cave.

The entrance was shaped like the letter A—a great dark mouth in the rock. Inside, the air was cold and damp. The main passage was narrow, with high limestone walls that rose sixty feet overhead. Every few yards, smaller corridors branched off in every direction. The cave was a labyrinth—miles and miles of twisting, intersecting tunnels that led nowhere and everywhere. People said you could wander for days and never find the end.

Most of the kids knew the first section of the cave and didn't venture beyond it. Groups chased each other through the dim passages, screaming and laughing, their candles throwing wild shadows on the walls. Tom and Becky ran with the rest, ducking through narrow openings, hiding in crevices, trying to scare each other.

Eventually they wandered away from the main group. They found themselves in a part of the cave they'd never seen before—no names scratched in the walls, no candle-smoke graffiti. Just raw, untouched stone.

Tom was thrilled. He wanted to explore. They followed the passage deeper and deeper, stopping to admire a cavern full of glittering stalactites, a crystal-clear underground spring, a vast room with pillars of stone where the ceiling dripped with thousands of sleeping bats.

Page 4

📚 Reading: THE PICNIC

Part 2 of 3

The bats were a problem. When the candlelight disturbed them, they came shrieking down in swarms. Tom grabbed Becky's hand and they ran, ducking into side passages to escape. When the bats were finally gone, they stopped to catch their breath.

"Tom," Becky said, "I haven't heard the others in a long time."

"Come to think of it—neither have I. We're pretty far down from them."

"How long have we been walking?"

"I don't know. We should probably start heading back."

"Can you find the way?"

But after ten minutes of walking, nothing looked familiar. After twenty minutes, Tom wasn't talking as cheerfully. After thirty minutes, Becky noticed.

"Tom, you didn't make any marks, did you?"

Page 5

📚 Reading: THE PICNIC

Part 3 of 3

His silence was answer enough.

"We're lost," Becky whispered.

"No—I'll figure it out. Come on."

But he couldn't figure it out. The passages all looked the same. He tried one direction, then another, then another. Nothing.

Becky started to cry. "We're lost, Tom! We're never going to get out!"

Tom felt cold terror creeping up his spine. But he put his arm around her and said: "We'll find the way. Don't give up."

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. What causes Tom and Becky to become lost in the cave?

2. What is the most frightening moment for the children in this chapter?

3. When Becky realizes they are lost, she says, "We're lost! We never can get out of this awful place! Oh, why did we ever leave the others!" How does Becky's reaction compare to Tom's? What does this scene reveal about how each character handles fear?

Page 7

📝 Grammar Workshop

Appositives

What is it?
An appositive is a noun or noun phrase that renames another noun right beside it. 'Tom, a clever boy, tricked his friends.' Appositives add detail without extra sentences. They're usually set off with commas.

Example:
"Tom's friend, Ben Rogers, fell for the trick."

✍️ Your Turn:

Write five sentences about characters or places from today's chapter. Each sentence should include an appositive that renames a noun. Use commas correctly.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 5

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

Create a different ending for today's chapter. What else could have happened?

💡 Tips: Start with a topic sentence, include specific details, and end with a conclusion.

Day 24: Lesson 24 - 6th Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 24

6th Grade ELA

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer

Chapter 20 • Week 5

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 24!

Today you will:

  • Read Chapter 20: HUCK SAVES THE WIDOW
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Missouri in the 1840s - Part 6"
  • Practice language skills
  • Complete journal activity

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

📚 Reading: HUCK SAVES THE WIDOW

Part 1 of 3

While Tom and Becky wandered deeper into the cave, Huck Finn was keeping watch outside the tavern.

The night was cloudy and still. By ten o'clock, the town had gone to sleep. By eleven, the tavern lights went dark. Huck waited in the shadows, patient and alert. Midnight came and went. He was about to give up when—

The alley door clicked open.

Two figures slipped out. One carried something bulky under his arm. The treasure box! Huck's pulse hammered. He couldn't go get Tom now—they'd lose the men forever. He had to follow them alone.

He crept after them through the dark streets, bare feet silent on the dirt. The men turned up the cross-street, climbed the path toward Cardiff Hill, passed the old Welshman's house, and kept climbing. Huck stayed close, hidden in the shadows.

They stopped at the top of the hill, near the path that led to the Widow Douglas's house. Huck crouched behind a boulder, straining to hear.

Page 4

📚 Reading: HUCK SAVES THE WIDOW

Part 2 of 3

"There are lights on," said the stranger. "She's got company."

Then Injun Joe spoke, and what he said turned Huck's blood to ice.

"Her husband was the justice who had me horsewhipped in front of the whole town, like a dog. He's dead now, so I can't get him. But I'll take it out on her."

"Don't kill her, Joe."

"I'm not going to kill her. I'm going to hurt her. Cut her face so she'll remember."

Huck had heard enough. He turned, crept backward with agonizing care—one step, then another, then another—and then he ran. He ran down the hill like his life depended on it, because it did, and he didn't stop until he was pounding on the Welshman's door.

Page 5

📚 Reading: HUCK SAVES THE WIDOW

Part 3 of 3

"Huckleberry Finn! Let me in—quick!"

"Huckleberry Finn? That's not a name that opens many doors. But let him in, boys."

Huck tumbled inside, gasping. "Please don't ever tell I was the one who told. He'd kill me. But the Widow Douglas—Injun Joe—he's up there right now—he's going to—"

The old Welshman didn't need to hear more. He grabbed his gun, woke his two grown sons, and within three minutes they were charging up the hill.

Huck hid behind a boulder and listened. For a terrible stretch of silence, nothing happened. Then—BANG! BANG! Gunshots split the night, followed by shouts and the sound of men crashing through the bushes.

Huck didn't wait to find out what happened. He ran back down the hill and didn't stop until he was three miles away.

Page 6

💭 Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter.

1. Why does Huck follow the two men instead of going to get Tom?

2. What does Huck do when he hears Injun Joe's plan to hurt the Widow Douglas?

3. Huck is terrified of Injun Joe, yet he risks his life to save the Widow Douglas. What motivates Huck to act despite his fear? What does this moment reveal about who Huck really is underneath his rough exterior?

Page 7

🗣️ Language Skills

Dialect and Regional Language

Learn:
Tom Sawyer includes dialect—regional ways of speaking. Jim and other characters use Southern American English dialect from the 1840s. Twain writes it phonetically to show how it sounded. Understanding dialect reveals character, setting, and social class.

Example:
"Standard: 'I cannot do that, Master Tom.' / Dialect: 'I dasn't do that, Mars Tom.' (dasn't = dare not, Mars = Master)"

✍️ Your Turn:

Find three examples of dialect in today's chapter. For each: write the dialect quote, translate it into standard English, and explain what the dialect tells you about the character who speaks it.

Page 8

📰 Informational Text

Missouri in the 1840s - Part 6

The Adventures of Tom Sawyer provides a window into American life during a pivotal period in the nation's history. Through Tom's adventures, readers learn about the values, challenges, and dreams of people living in small-town America during the mid-19th century.

Mark Twain drew heavily from his own childhood experiences in Hannibal, Missouri, making the novel a valuable historical document as well as entertaining fiction. The customs, language, and social structures depicted in the novel reflect authentic details of the time period.

Understanding this historical context helps readers appreciate both the timeless themes of childhood and adventure, as well as the specific cultural moment Twain captured in his writing.

Life along the Mississippi River shaped the culture of towns like St. Petersburg. The river provided commerce, transportation, and a sense of connection to the wider world. Steamboats were technological marvels of the age, and becoming a pilot was among the most prestigious careers available.

0 / 30 words

0 / 30 words

0 / 30 words

Page 9

What emotion did you feel most while reading today's chapter? Describe why the story made you feel that way.

💭 This is your space to explore your ideas and reactions to the story.

Page 7

Assessment Complete!
